metrics.narrative: Cadence has articulated clear operational pain and a forward volume estimate but no hard dollar ROI. On the 25 Feb discovery call the buyer named success metrics as a seamless end-to-end solution serving procurement, legal, and sales, efficient transaction throughput, AI-driven agreement creation, and strict infosec compliance, and flagged that renewal metrics are tracked manually today. On the 13 Apr pricing/volume sync Peter Hughes referenced an internal estimate of ~12,500 annually for go-forward contract volume, and a legacy repository of roughly 500,000 documents to migrate — the quantitative anchor for value. No cost-per-contract, cycle-time, or headcount savings figure has been stated, so the ROI case is not yet quantified. Quantifying time-saved-per-contract x 12,500 contracts is the open move and the spine of the C-level value story.

champion.narrative: No confirmed champion advocating Zycus over Ironclad; the strongest relationship is a coach, not a champion. Peter Hughes (Senior Category Manager, named CLM project lead in the 26 May Next Step) is the most engaged buyer contact, appearing across intake, pricing, workshop and POC-framework calls and driving the sandbox-access ask, but he coordinates neutrally — no C-level introductions, no shared head-to-head POC feedback, no push-back on Ironclad for Zycus. What has developed: a working, responsive relationship and a recent inbound (Devika introduced the owner and invited him to Palo Alto). What is missing: a named internal advocate who will argue for Zycus to budget-approving leadership. A 1:1 with Peter to debrief the POC, learn the Zycus-vs-Ironclad standing, and have him broker the approver intro is the priority move.

sources: SF Next Step (09 Jun, down to 1 of 2 incl. Ironclad)
competition.narrative: This is a two-finalist bake-off, Zycus vs Ironclad. Ironclad is the single live peer threat: the legal team's AI appetite and admiration for iClad was stated 23 Jan, and the 09 Jun Next Step confirms the field is down to 1 of 2 vendors including Ironclad. Both ran parallel 5-day POC sandboxes in early May. The displaced incumbents are Coupa (buy-side), the homegrown Salesforce/iManage/Word stack, and DocuSign. Zycus wins by leading with enterprise breadth (one platform across procurement + legal + sales, native SAP/Coupa/Tableau/Salesforce integration, Merlin AI + Teams intake) against Ironclad's legal-AI-depth positioning. The gap: this differentiation has not been activated with the budget approver, and no post-POC head-to-head scorecard has been obtained.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is confirmed and specific. Cadence runs CLM on Coupa for buy-side plus a homegrown stack (Salesforce for NDA intake, iManage repository, Word, DocuSign) with no standardized intake orchestration. On 25 Feb the buyer described disparate intake methods causing inconsistent workflows and extra manual effort, renewal metrics tracked manually, and no standardized metadata capture or orchestration. The legal team has pressing AI requirements (23 Jan). As a second-tier defense contractor they have hard InfoSec/data-residency obligations. Pain owners: procurement (Devesh/Jason), legal (Melanie), sales-finance workflow.

sources: SF Next Step (21 Apr MSA submitted)
paper_process.narrative: Contracting is partially in motion but redlines are not visibly progressing — and this is now a live close-risk. The MSA was submitted 21 Apr (Completed) to start parallel legal review. InfoSec diligence is substantially advanced from the 06 Apr IT session. However, no redline session or SOW scoping appears on any recorded call between 22 Apr and today, and the legal contacts (Melanie Hamm, Dave Bebb, Issac Lin) have not surfaced open redline points. With a mid-July decision named and no paper owner driving the redline/SOW sequence, contracting is the most likely tie-break to lose this deal on execution risk. Booking a legal + procurement redline/SOW sync is the move to de-risk it.

sources: SF Next Step (26 May, leadership budget meeting)
economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer has appeared on any of the 13 deep-read calls. Devesh Chadha (Director - Procurement COE, Decision Maker) controls the RFP, budget conversation and timeline, but he is a decision maker, not the signing economic buyer on a $210k multi-year commitment. The Next Step (26 May) references a Cadence leadership meeting to listen to final budget requests, confirming a leadership/CFO budget layer exists that has not engaged Zycus directly. The 5-year term with annual upfront payment makes that sign-off material. The path to that approver is through the champion, not a cold approach — identifying and getting a 30-minute briefing with the approver, brokered by Peter, is the single biggest power gap.

decision_process.narrative: Cadence ran a formal CLM RFP from fall 2025, moved through demos to a finalist shortlist (Zycus confirmed a finalist on 01 Apr), then ran parallel 5-day POC sandboxes for both finalists in early May. The committee spans procurement (Devesh, Peter, Jason), legal (Melanie, Dave, Issac) and IT. Per Next Step the decision slipped from early June to mid-July and now routes through a leadership budget meeting. The approval chain above Devesh is the one unmapped link — and the choke point on a credible close date.

decision_criteria.narrative: The evaluation criteria are well documented. On 25 Feb the buyer set the bar as one platform serving procurement, legal and sales, AI-driven agreement creation, SAP/DocuSign/Salesforce integration, and strict InfoSec compliance. The 06 Apr IT architecture session deepened this into multi-tenant isolation, SAML/OpenID auth, SOC1/SOC2, BCP/backup, and SAP ECC6 / iManage / Salesforce integration. The 20-21 Apr legal workshops surfaced dynamic conditional clause logic by license type, sales-finance access control, and Salesforce-initiated/Zycus-authored workflow. Criteria are confirmed and granular; the open question is how Zycus scored vs Ironclad on them in the POC.

deltas.to: Ironclad is the single live peer threat in a two-finalist bake-off, with strong legal-AI brand and explicit buyer endorsement ('Legal team has pressing AI requi
deltas.subject: Ironclad is the single live peer threat in a two-finalist bake-off, with strong legal-AI brand and explicit buyer endorsement ('Legal team has pressing AI requirements and has been impressed by tools like iClad' per 23 Jan intro call). Both vendors ran parallel 5-day POC sandboxes early May, and the decision has slipped to mid-July (9 Jun Next Step). No competitive debrief or head-to-head scorecard shared post-POC, and no champion advocating for Zycus over Ironclad. Zycus positioning (enterprise breadth: procurement + legal + sales CLM, SAP/Coupa/Tableau integration, Merlin AI + intake orchestration) vs. Ironclad positioning (legal-AI depth) has not been activated in a final C-level presentation. Risk: committee decides on feature/price parity or defaults to Ironclad's legal-AI brand in the absence of a Zycus champion. Mitigation: (1) Schedule 1:1 with Peter Hughes (project lead) to surface POC comparative feedback and internal champion; (2) request C-level executive briefing to present Zycus as the enterprise-wide AI platform (not just legal) and differentiate vs. Ironclad's legal-only positioning; (3) quantify ROI anchored to 12,500 annual contracts (enterprise-wide unification + AI time savings) to make the financial case for Zycus breadth over Ironclad depth.

deltas.to: C-level sponsor (likely CFO or COO) unnamed and off-call in a $210k, multi-year SaaS buy. Devesh Chadha (Director—Procurement COE, Decision Maker Contact Role)
deltas.subject: C-level sponsor (likely CFO or COO) unnamed and off-call in a $210k, multi-year SaaS buy. Devesh Chadha (Director—Procurement COE, Decision Maker Contact Role) controls the RFP and budget, but no C-level exec has joined any recorded session. The 26 May Next Step references 'Candence leadership meeting this week to listen to final budget requests'—the EB/CFO layer exists but has not engaged directly with the relevant stakeholder a two-finalist bake-off where both vendors are likely in final negotiation and the decision has slipped to mid-July (9 Jun Next Step), the C-level sponsor will make the final call, and Zycus has no relationship with them. Risk: C-level defaults to feature/price parity, Ironclad's brand, or risk-aversion (choose the known legal-AI vendor over the broader but less-known enterprise platform) in the absence of a direct Zycus engagement. Mitigation: Request a 30-minute executive briefing for the C-level approving sponsor (CFO or COO, likely the exec from the 26 May leadership meeting) to present the consolidated business case: (a) quantified ROI anchored to 12,500 annual contracts (time saved per contract × volume = cost avoidance); (b) Zycus as the enterprise-wide platform (procurement + legal + sales CLM, SAP/Coupa/Tableau integration, Merlin AI + intake orchestration) vs. Ironclad as a legal-only point solution that leaves procurement and sales in the old fragmented state; (c) secure explicit buy-in and address any C-level concerns (budget, risk, timeline) before mid-July decision. This briefing must happen in the next 10 days (by 6 Jul) to influence the final approval.

deltas.to: No confirmed champion in a two-finalist, committee-driven enterprise buy. Peter Hughes (Senior Category Manager, Evaluator Contact Role, 'CLM project led' per 2
deltas.subject: No confirmed champion in a two-finalist, committee-driven enterprise buy. Peter Hughes (Senior Category Manager, Evaluator Contact Role, 'CLM project led' per 26 May Next Step) is the closest—attended 10 of 17 calls, drove sandbox access request (28 Apr)—but has shown neutral coordination, not advocacy (no introductions to C-level, no shared internal competitive analysis, no push-back on Ironclad for Zycus, no driving urgency). Inference: Peter is a coach (provides intel, coordinates logistics), not a champion (does not advocate for Zycus over Ironclad). Devesh Chadha (Decision Maker), Melanie Hamm (legal lead), and Jason Lieu (procurement operations) are all engaged and responsive but none has demonstrated active advocacy. Champion development stalled post-POC—no 1:1 champion-building session, no exec sponsor intro, no internal-doc share. Risk: deal decided purely on feature/price parity by committee vote, with no champion to break a tie or accelerate the close. In a two-finalist bake-off with strong competitor (Ironclad) and no internal advocate, Zycus is vulnerable to losing on committee consensus or risk-aversion. Mitigation: Schedule a 30-minute 1:1 with Peter Hughes (the project lead and likely coach) in the next 7 days (by 3 Jul) to (a) debrief the POC—what went well, what gaps remain, how does Zycus compare to Ironclad in his view; (b) ask directly: 'Who internally is championing this project and will advocate for the winning vendor to the C-level sponsor? If that person is not yet identified, how do we position Zycus to win that advocacy?'; (c) request an introduction to that internal advocate or, if none exists, offer to co-develop the business case with Peter to present to the C-level layer. This session is the single highest-leverage move to surface or build the champion before mid-July decision.

deltas.to: Close date 10 Jul is not credible; decision pushed to mid-July per 9 Jun Next Step. Remaining gates (vendor selection, EB/CFO sign-off, MSA/SOW redlines, InfoSe
deltas.subject: Close date 10 Jul is not credible; decision pushed to mid-July per 9 Jun Next Step. Remaining gates (vendor selection, EB/CFO sign-off, MSA/SOW redlines, InfoSec final sign-off, final negotiation) cannot complete by 10 Jul. Forward slip of ~2–3 weeks required. The mid-July slip (from anticipated early Jun vendor selection per 26 May Next Step to mid-Jul decision per 9 Jun Next Step) is a timing adjustment, not a stall—buyer is still engaged, POC completed, leadership budget review occurred week of 26 May—but the date is indefensible. Risk: close date slips to late Jul or early Aug if C-level sign-off, legal redlines, or final negotiation extend beyond mid-Jul. Forecast_category Best Case is indefensible (no confirmed champion, no EB on-call engagement post-POC, mid-Jul decision = Pipeline is the honest category until vendor selection completes and EB sign-off is secured). Mitigation: (1) Accelerate the C-level executive briefing (by 6 Jul) to secure buy-in and shorten approval cycle; (2) schedule legal + procurement sync (by 3 Jul) to close MSA redlines and SOW scoping; (3) request explicit mid-Jul target date from Peter Hughes or Devesh Chadha and align final deliverables (proposal, references, contract finalization) to that date.

deltas.to: MSA submitted 21 Apr (Next Step: 'Submit MSA. (Completed)') but no redline session or SOW scoping discussed post-submission. Legal redlines and SOW structure re
deltas.subject: MSA submitted 21 Apr (Next Step: 'Submit MSA. (Completed)') but no redline session or SOW scoping discussed post-submission. Legal redlines and SOW structure remain unresolved 65 days post-MSA (21 Apr to 26 Jun). InfoSec diligence confirmed (6 Apr IT architecture session: SOC1/2, multi-tenant isolation, BCP/backup, data-migration security), but legal contract finalization has not progressed on any recorded call 22 Apr–26 Jun. The mid-July decision slip may reflect legal/InfoSec review time or final redline negotiation. Risk: contract complexity or unresolved redlines become a tie-breaker in the two-finalist bake-off—if Ironclad's MSA is simpler or faster to close, Cadence may choose them on execution risk. Procurement may also surface pricing/terms concerns (line-item bundling, migration cost, annual volume projections) during final contract review that were not resolved in the 7 Apr pricing call. Mitigation: Schedule a 30-minute legal + procurement sync with Melanie Hamm, Dave Bebb (legal), and Jason Lieu (procurement) in the next 7 days (by 3 Jul) to (a) review MSA redline status—any concerns, open points, or blockers; (b) agree on SOW structure (implementation timeline, data migration scope, support SLA, pricing finalization); (c) surface and resolve any contract friction before mid-July decision. Position Zycus as the vendor who makes contracting seamless vs. a competitor who adds legal complexity.

This session is the single highest-leverage move to surface or build champion before mid-July decision.
